region_of_origin: Mediterranean origin; widely cultivated and available in Singapore supermarkets
food_subgroup: Cruciferous vegetables, raw; white cauliflower head, as sold

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for raw cauliflower food product in available reference; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 100g reference portion of raw white cauliflower florets with stems, as sold in Singapore supermarkets.

nutrient_source.title: Cauliflower, Raw — USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID 170397)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Direct match to USDA SR Legacy entry for raw cauliflower, FDC ID 170397. All per-100g nutrient values sourced from this entry.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Cauliflower
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~15 for cauliflower is widely cited in peer-reviewed literature and the University of Sydney GI Database. Available carbohydrate per 100g = 5.0g total carbohydrate minus 2.0g dietary fibre = 3.0g. GL = (15 × 3.0) ÷ 100 = 0.45, rounded to 0.5 per 100g reference portion.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (30mg/100g) and high potassium (299mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Cauliflower is highly suitable for hypertension management: sodium content is very low at 30mg per 100g, and potassium at 299mg per 100g supports vascular tone and blood pressure regulation consistent with DASH dietary principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low GI (~15) and very low glycemic load (~0.5 per 100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cauliflower is an excellent choice for Type 2 Diabetes management: GI ~15 and GL ~0.5 per 100g minimise postprandial glucose excursions, and its use as a cauliflower rice substitute reduces GL from approximately 37 (per 150g white rice serving) to approximately 2 per equivalent volume, representing a clinically meaningful reduction in glycaemic exposure.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.1g/100g), zero cholesterol, high vitamin C (48.2mg/100g), and folate (57mcg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Cauliflower is cardioprotective: saturated fat is negligible at 0.1g per 100g, cholesterol is zero, and vitamin C (48.2mg) and folate (57mcg) support endothelial function and homocysteine metabolism, making it a suitable daily vegetable for cardiovascular disease prevention diets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Low vitamin K relative to broccoli/kale; folate relevant for methotrexate patients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cauliflower is suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients: its low vitamin K content (~15.5mcg/100g) makes it less warfarin-relevant than broccoli or kale, folate (57mcg/100g) is clinically relevant for patients on methotrexate, and its very low calorie density supports weight management which reduces mechanical joint load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cauliflower (Raw) is a white cruciferous vegetable consumed raw or cooked, comprising compact white florets with firm stems. Per 100g raw: 25 kcal (one of the lowest calorie densities among vegetables), 1.9g protein, 0.3g fat, 5.0g carbohydrate, 2.0g dietary fibre, GI ~15, GL ~0.5. Vitamin C is notably high at 48.2mg (approximately 80% of adult RDA). Sodium is very low at 30mg; potassium is 299mg. Key clinical application: cauliflower rice (riced or grated raw cauliflower) provides approximately 25 kcal per 100g versus approximately 130 kcal per 100g for cooked white rice — an 80% calorie reduction — with GI dropping from ~72 (white rice) to ~15, making it a practical and evidence-supported diabetes meal planning strategy. Pre-riced cauliflower is available at NTUC FairPrice and Cold Storage in Singapore. Low vitamin K (~15.5mcg/100g) makes it less warfarin-relevant than broccoli or kale. Folate at 57mcg per 100g is relevant for patients on methotrexate. Source: USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 170397. Confidence: high.
common_user_queries: cauliflower rice vs white rice nutrition
is cauliflower good for high blood pressure

confidence_reason: All per-100g nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 170397, an approved primary source with exact match to raw cauliflower. GI value of ~15 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. No estimation or adaptation required for core macros or secondary nutrients.
notes: Vitamin A (RAE) is 0.0 mcg per 100g: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy FDC 170397 — raw white cauliflower contains negligible beta-carotene. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 mcg: naturally zero as cauliflower is a plant food containing no cobalamin. Vitamin D is 0.0 mcg: naturally zero as cauliflower is not a dietary source of vitamin D. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg: naturally zero as this is a plant food. Trans fat is 0.0g: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. Monounsaturated fat (0.017g) and polyunsaturated fat (0.015g) are minor values reported in USDA SR Legacy FDC 170397. Water content (92.07g/100g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC 170397. No -1 sentinel values used; all nutrients are reported or confirmed from the approved source. GI source is analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care peer-reviewed publication. SNOMED CT code not identified; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient for ontological alignment. Record type: generic raw vegetable reference, 100g standard portion.
